text: What is the most streamed song on Spotify of all time?
hint: Released in 2019, it soundtracked countless TikTok videos and a Super Bowl halftime show.

text: Which actress played Katniss Everdeen in The Hunger Games film series?
hint: She was 20 years old when the first film was released in 2012.

text: What reality TV show features contestants stranded in the wilderness competing for $1 million?
hint: Its first season aired in 2000 and was set in Borneo.

text: Which K-pop group released the global hit 'Dynamite' in 2020?
hint: It was their first all-English language single and debuted at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100.

text: In what year did the MCU film Avengers: Endgame release?
hint: It became the highest-grossing film of all time, surpassing Avatar briefly.

text: Which social media platform is known for its disappearing Stories feature, originally pioneered before Instagram copied it?
hint: It launched in 2011 and was famously turned down by Facebook's Mark Zuckerberg before he built a copy.

text: Who played Tony Stark / Iron Man in the Marvel Cinematic Universe?
hint: He kicked off the MCU in 2008 with the first Iron Man film.

text: Which TV series featured the fictional Dunder Mifflin paper company?
hint: The American version ran from 2005 to 2013 and was based on a British show of the same name.

text: What is the name of Taylor Swift's re-recorded version of her 2008 album Fearless?
hint: She re-recorded her first six albums after a dispute over master recordings with Scooter Braun.

text: Which sport does LeBron James play professionally?
hint: He was drafted first overall in 2003 and has won four NBA championships.

text: What streaming service originally produced the series Stranger Things?
hint: The show is set in the 1980s and features a group of kids in Hawkins, Indiana.

text: Which rapper's real name is Aubrey Drake Graham?
hint: He first gained fame as an actor on the Canadian TV show Degrassi.

text: What film franchise features the character Jack Sparrow?
hint: Johnny Depp's portrayal became iconic, and the first film was based on a Disney theme park ride.

text: Which pop star performed the halftime show at Super Bowl LVII in 2023?
hint: It was her first major performance in years and she revealed her pregnancy during it.

text: What is the name of the fictional land in the TV show Game of Thrones?
hint: The show is based on George R.R. Martin's A Song of Ice and Fire book series.

text: Which YouTuber is known for the phrase 'Subscribe to PewDiePie'?
hint: He was the most subscribed individual creator on YouTube for many years.

text: What Marvel character is associated with the phrase 'With great power comes great responsibility'?
hint: This line is most associated with his Uncle Ben.

text: Which actress starred as Mia in La La Land (2016)?
hint: She won the Academy Award for Best Actress for the role.

text: In which city do the fictional characters from Friends live?
hint: They frequently hang out at a coffee shop called Central Perk.

text: What song by Olivia Rodrigo was her debut single, released in January 2021?
hint: Written about a breakup, it smashed streaming records and topped charts worldwide.

text: Which company owns Instagram and WhatsApp?
hint: The parent company rebranded in October 2021.

text: What sport does Serena Williams play?
hint: She has won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, the most by any player in the Open Era.

text: Which Netflix show follows a group of women in a women's prison and is based on a memoir?
hint: Based on Piper Kerman's memoir about her time at a federal correctional facility.

text: What is the name of the song that includes the lyrics 'Just a small town girl, livin' in a lonely world'?
hint: Released in 1981, it became a karaoke and sports anthem staple.

text: Which actor plays the character Sherlock Holmes in the BBC series Sherlock?
hint: He went on to play Doctor Strange in the Marvel Cinematic Universe.

text: What year did the original Star Wars film (A New Hope) release?
hint: Directed by George Lucas, it was initially considered a risky passion project.

text: Which pop group sang 'Wannabe', their debut single from 1996?
hint: The group consisted of five members each with a 'Spice' nickname.

text: What is the highest-grossing animated film of all time?
hint: This photorealistic remake far surpassed the original animated version at the box office.

text: Which show features the Pearson family in storylines spanning multiple decades?
hint: The NBC drama premiered in 2016 and starred Milo Ventimiglia and Mandy Moore.

text: What nickname is given to the Oscar statuette?
hint: The golden figure officially depicts a knight holding a sword, standing on a reel of film.

text: Who directed the 2014 film Birdman?
hint: The film appeared to be shot in a single continuous take and starred Michael Keaton.

text: What video game franchise features characters named Master Chief and Cortana?
hint: Developed by Bungie and later 343 Industries, it was a flagship Xbox title.

text: Which singer is known as the 'Queen of Pop'?
hint: She has sold over 300 million records worldwide and had hits from the 1980s through the 2000s.

text: In which 2010 film does Leonardo DiCaprio plant ideas in people's dreams?
hint: Directed by Christopher Nolan, its spinning top ending became one of cinema's great debates.

text: What is the name of the coffee shop in the TV series Friends?
hint: The group has a suspiciously permanent couch there despite it being a bustling NYC café.

text: Which artist released the album '25' featuring the hit song 'Hello'?
hint: Released in 2015, 'Hello' sold over a million digital copies in its first week in the US.

text: What is the name of the fictional school in Harry Potter?
answer: Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry
hint: It is located in Scotland and divided into four houses named after its founders.

text: Which actor plays the Joker in the 2019 DC film Joker?
hint: He won the Academy Award for Best Actor for the role.

text: What sport is associated with the annual Wimbledon tournament?
hint: It is the oldest Grand Slam tournament, played on grass courts in London.

text: Which animated Disney film features a character named Elsa with ice powers?
hint: Released in 2013, its song 'Let It Go' became a global phenomenon.

text: What is the name of Jon Snow's direwolf in Game of Thrones?
hint: Unlike other Stark direwolves, Ghost is an albino with red eyes.

text: Who created the social network Facebook?
hint: He started it from his Harvard dorm room in 2004 with several co-founders.

text: Which film features the line 'Life is like a box of chocolates'?
hint: Tom Hanks won the Academy Award for Best Actor for this role in 1994.

text: What dance move did Michael Jackson popularize, seeming to walk forward while moving backward?
hint: He debuted it publicly during a performance of 'Billie Jean' in 1983.

text: Which TV show features a chemistry teacher who turns to making methamphetamine?
hint: The show stars Bryan Cranston as Walter White and Aaron Paul as Jesse Pinkman.

text: What app uses a flame streak to show how many consecutive days you've used it?
hint: It's a language-learning app whose owl mascot has become a meme for its persistence.

text: Which 2022 film starring Austin Butler is a biopic about a legendary rock musician?
hint: Directed by Baz Luhrmann, it depicts the life of Elvis Presley.

text: What is the name of the fictional kingdom in the animated film Tangled?
hint: It's a Disney adaptation of the fairy tale Rapunzel, released in 2010.

text: Which soccer player is commonly nicknamed 'CR7'?
hint: The CR stands for his name and 7 is his iconic jersey number.

text: What is the human body's largest organ?
hint: It covers the entire external surface and serves as a barrier against the environment.

text: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
hint: It comes from the Latin word 'aurum'.

text: How many planets are in our solar system?
hint: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006 by the International Astronomical Union.

text: What is the speed of light in a vacuum, approximately?
hint: In round numbers, it's about 186,000 miles per second.

text: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
hint: They use it along with sunlight and water to produce glucose and oxygen.

text: What is the powerhouse of the cell?
hint: These organelles generate most of the cell's supply of ATP, used as a source of chemical energy.

text: What is the atomic number of carbon?
hint: Carbon has 6 protons and is the basis of all known life on Earth.

text: What planet is known as the Red Planet?
hint: Its reddish appearance is due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

text: What is the name of the process by which water changes from a liquid to a gas?
hint: At 100°C at sea level, this becomes the rapid process known as boiling.

text: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
hint: It makes up about 78% of the atmosphere; oxygen is second at around 21%.

text: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
hint: Named after German physicist Georg Simon Ohm, who formulated Ohm's Law.

text: Which bone is the longest in the human body?
hint: It runs from the hip to the knee and can bear up to 30 times your body weight.

text: What theory describes the origin of the universe approximately 13.8 billion years ago?
hint: It proposes the universe expanded from an extremely hot, dense singularity.

text: What is the name of the DNA structure discovered by Watson and Crick?
hint: Their 1953 paper described the twisted ladder shape, aided by Rosalind Franklin's X-ray images.

text: What is the chemical formula for water?
hint: It consists of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom.

text: What is the term for the splitting of an atomic nucleus, releasing large amounts of energy?
hint: It is the process that powers nuclear reactors and atomic bombs.

text: What planet has the most moons in our solar system?
hint: As of 2023, Saturn has over 140 confirmed moons, surpassing Jupiter.

text: What is the name for the negatively charged particles that orbit an atom's nucleus?
hint: They exist in shells or energy levels and are responsible for chemical bonding.

text: Which vitamin is produced by the skin when exposed to sunlight?
hint: It's essential for calcium absorption and bone health.

text: What is the name of the force that keeps planets in orbit around the sun?
hint: Isaac Newton famously described this universal force after observing a falling apple.

text: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
hint: It rates 10 on the Mohs hardness scale and is a form of pure carbon.

text: What type of rock is formed from cooled lava or magma?
hint: Examples include granite and basalt.

text: What is the study of living organisms called?
hint: It is divided into many sub-disciplines including zoology, botany, and microbiology.

text: What is the name of the first artificial satellite launched into Earth's orbit?
hint: It was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.

text: How many chambers does the human heart have?
hint: Two atria receive blood and two ventricles pump it out.

text: What is the boiling point of water at standard atmospheric pressure in Celsius?
hint: At higher altitudes, lower air pressure causes water to boil at lower temperatures.

text: What gas is released when acids react with metals?
hint: This flammable gas is why you should be careful when mixing acids with metals.

text: What is the name of the protective ozone layer and where is it located?
answer: The ozone layer, in the stratosphere
hint: It absorbs most of the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation.

text: What is the process by which plants lose water through their leaves?
hint: It occurs mainly through tiny pores called stomata and helps drive water uptake from roots.

text: What is Newton's second law of motion?
answer: Force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma)
hint: It explains why a heavier object requires more force to accelerate at the same rate.

text: What is the name of the galaxy that contains our solar system?
hint: Our solar system is located in one of its spiral arms, about 26,000 light-years from the galactic center.

text: What element has the symbol 'Fe' on the periodic table?
hint: The symbol comes from the Latin word 'ferrum'.

text: What is the name of the scale used to measure earthquake magnitude?
hint: Each whole number increase represents a tenfold increase in amplitude.

text: What is the smallest unit of matter?
hint: Though subatomic particles like quarks are smaller, the atom is the smallest unit that retains an element's chemical properties.

text: What is the term for an organism that makes its own food using sunlight?
hint: Plants, algae, and some bacteria are examples; they form the base of food chains.

text: What is the name of the space telescope launched in 1990 that orbits Earth?
hint: After a flawed mirror was corrected by astronauts in 1993, it began delivering stunning images.

text: Which planet has the Great Red Spot, a storm larger than Earth?
hint: This anticyclonic storm has been observed for at least 350 years.

text: What is the name for the group of proteins that catalyze chemical reactions in living organisms?
hint: They lower the activation energy needed for reactions; amylase in saliva is one example.

text: What type of radiation has the highest frequency in the electromagnetic spectrum?
hint: They are produced by radioactive decay and nuclear explosions and are highly penetrating.

text: What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
hint: It comes from the Latin 'natrium'; sodium is essential for nerve function.

text: What is the name of the first human to walk on the Moon?
hint: He stepped onto the lunar surface on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.

text: What is the name of the phenomenon where light bends as it passes from one medium to another?
hint: It's why a straw appears bent when placed in a glass of water.

text: What organ produces insulin in the human body?
hint: Insulin regulates blood sugar levels; failure to produce it leads to Type 1 diabetes.

text: What is the term for the transfer of heat through direct contact?
hint: Metals are good conductors; wood is a poor conductor (insulator).

text: What is the largest planet in our solar system?
hint: It is so large that all other planets in the solar system could fit inside it.

text: What is the name for the RNA molecule that carries genetic information from DNA to ribosomes?
hint: mRNA vaccines, like those for COVID-19, use this molecule to instruct cells to produce a protein.

text: What is the name of the process by which a liquid becomes a solid?
hint: Water freezes at 0°C at standard atmospheric pressure.

text: What is the most abundant element in the universe?
hint: It makes up about 75% of all normal matter by mass in the universe.

text: Which country is the largest by land area?
hint: It spans 11 time zones and covers about 17 million square kilometres.

text: What is the capital city of Australia?
hint: It was purpose-built as a compromise between Sydney and Melbourne, both of which wanted to be the capital.

text: What is the longest river in the world?
hint: It flows northward through northeastern Africa and empties into the Mediterranean Sea.

text: On which continent is the Sahara Desert located?
hint: It is the world's largest hot desert, covering most of North Africa.

text: What is the tallest mountain in the world?
hint: It is located in the Himalayas on the border of Nepal and Tibet, standing at 8,848.86 metres.

text: What ocean is the largest in the world?
hint: It covers more than 30% of Earth's surface — larger than all land combined.

text: What is the capital of Japan?
hint: It is the most populous metropolitan area in the world with over 37 million people.

text: What country has the most natural lakes in the world?
hint: It contains more than 60% of the world's lakes, including the Great Lakes shared with the US.

text: What is the smallest country in the world by land area?
hint: It is an independent city-state enclosed within Rome, Italy, and serves as the seat of the Catholic Church.

text: Through how many countries does the Amazon River flow?
answer: 9 countries (primarily Brazil, but also Peru, Colombia, and others)
hint: The vast majority of its length runs through Brazil.

text: What is the capital of Brazil?
hint: It was purpose-built and officially became the capital in 1960, replacing Rio de Janeiro.

text: Which mountain range separates Europe from Asia?
hint: They run north-south through Russia and are considered the traditional boundary between the two continents.

text: What is the name of the strait that separates Europe from Africa?
hint: It connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea and is only about 14 kilometres wide at its narrowest.

text: What is the capital of Canada?
hint: Not Toronto or Vancouver — this city in Ontario was chosen as a compromise in 1857.

text: Which African country has the largest population?
hint: With over 220 million people, it is also the most populous Black country in the world.

text: What is the name of the world's largest coral reef system?
hint: Located off the coast of Queensland, Australia, it is visible from space.

text: What country is home to the ancient city of Petra?
hint: Known as the Rose City for its pink sandstone cliffs, it's half-built and half-carved into rock.

text: What is the name of the sea between Italy and Croatia?
hint: It is an arm of the Mediterranean Sea and borders several Balkan nations.

text: Which country has the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites?
hint: With over 50 sites, it edges out China for the top spot.

text: What is the capital of South Africa?
answer: Pretoria (executive capital; Cape Town for legislative and Bloemfontein for judicial)
hint: South Africa uniquely has three capitals for three branches of government.

text: What country does the island of Greenland belong to?
hint: Despite being geographically closer to North America, Greenland is an autonomous territory of this Scandinavian nation.

text: What is the name of the largest desert in the world by area (including cold deserts)?
hint: Antarctica qualifies as a desert because it receives very little precipitation.

text: What river runs through Egypt and is essential to its civilization?
hint: Ancient Egyptians referred to the fertile Nile Delta region as the 'gift of the Nile'.

text: Which two countries share the longest international border in the world?
hint: The border stretches approximately 8,891 kilometres, largely along the 49th parallel.

text: What is the deepest lake in the world?
hint: Located in Siberia, Russia, it holds about 20% of the world's unfrozen fresh surface water.

text: What is the capital of New Zealand?
hint: It is located at the southern tip of the North Island and is the southernmost capital of a sovereign nation.

text: What sea borders both Israel and Jordan?
answer: The Red Sea (and the Dead Sea for Jordan-Israel)
hint: The Dead Sea is actually the salt lake that directly borders both countries.

text: Which country has the highest population in the world?
hint: It surpassed China around 2023 to become the world's most populous nation.

text: What is the name of the active volcano on the island of Hawaii?
hint: Kilauea is one of the most active volcanoes on Earth and has been erupting almost continuously since 1983.

text: What is the smallest continent by land area?
hint: It is also the only continent that is also a country, sometimes called Oceania to include Pacific islands.

text: Which country is known as the Land of the Rising Sun?
hint: The name 'Japan' in Japanese is Nihon, which translates roughly to 'origin of the sun'.

text: What large island lies to the southeast of the Indian subcontinent?
hint: Formerly known as Ceylon, it is known for its tea plantations and Buddhist temples.

text: What is the capital of Argentina?
hint: Its name means 'good winds' or 'fair winds' in Spanish.

text: Which European country has the most islands?
hint: It has approximately 221,800 islands along its coastlines and in its lakes.

text: What is the name of the large peninsula that includes Spain and Portugal?
hint: It is separated from the rest of Europe by the Pyrenees mountain range.

text: What is the name of the body of water between Saudi Arabia and Iran?
hint: Also called the Arabian Gulf by some Arab states, it is a strategic waterway for global oil transport.

text: What country contains the most of the Amazon Rainforest?
hint: About 60% of the Amazon rainforest is located within Brazil's borders.

text: What is the capital of Egypt?
hint: It is the largest city in Africa and the Arab world.

text: What mountain range is home to Mount Everest?
hint: The range spans five countries: Nepal, India, Bhutan, China, and Pakistan.

text: What is the name of the famous waterfall on the border of the USA and Canada?
hint: It consists of three separate waterfalls: Horseshoe, American, and Bridal Veil Falls.

text: What country is known as the 'Roof of the World'?
answer: Tibet (a region of China) / Nepal area
hint: The Tibetan Plateau averages over 4,500 metres above sea level.

text: What is the capital of Turkey?
hint: Many people mistakenly say Istanbul, but the capital moved to Ankara in 1923 when the Republic was founded.

text: What year did World War I begin?
hint: It was triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo.

text: Who was the first President of the United States?
hint: He served two terms from 1789 to 1797 and is often called the 'Father of His Country'.

text: What ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
hint: Also known as the Pharos of Alexandria, it guided sailors for centuries before being destroyed by earthquakes.

text: In which year did the Berlin Wall fall?
hint: East and West Germans tore it down on November 9, 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War.

text: Who was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize?
hint: She won the prize in Physics in 1903 and again in Chemistry in 1911 — the only person to win in two different sciences.

text: What empire was ruled by Julius Caesar?
answer: The Roman Republic (not yet Empire during Caesar's rule)
hint: Caesar was never officially Emperor; he held the title Dictator. His adopted son Augustus became the first Emperor.

text: What was the name of the ship that sank on its maiden voyage in 1912?
hint: It struck an iceberg in the North Atlantic Ocean on April 14–15, 1912.

text: Which country was the first to give women the right to vote?
hint: New Zealand granted women the right to vote in 1893, making it a pioneer of women's suffrage.

text: Who was the Egyptian pharaoh whose tomb was discovered by Howard Carter in 1922?
hint: He became pharaoh at about age 9 and died around age 19; his tomb was remarkably intact.

text: What was the name of the first artificial satellite, launched by the Soviet Union in 1957?
hint: Its launch shocked the Western world and kicked off the Space Race.

text: What event in 1929 triggered the Great Depression?
hint: The stock market collapsed on October 29, 1929, wiping out millions of investors.

text: Who led the Cuban Revolution in 1959?
hint: He overthrew dictator Fulgencio Batista and ruled Cuba until 2008.

text: What ancient city was famously buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD?
hint: The city was preserved in volcanic ash, providing an extraordinary snapshot of Roman life.

text: Who was the leader of Nazi Germany during World War II?
hint: He rose to power in 1933 as Chancellor and later declared himself Führer.

text: What declaration, issued in 1776, announced American independence from Britain?
hint: Primarily authored by Thomas Jefferson, it was adopted on July 4, 1776.

text: What was the name of the apartheid leader in South Africa who became its first Black president?
hint: He was imprisoned for 27 years before becoming president in 1994 after South Africa's first democratic elections.

text: What was the name of the ancient trade route connecting China to the Mediterranean?
hint: It was a network of trade and cultural transmission routes that operated from the 2nd century BCE.

text: In what year did the French Revolution begin?
hint: The storming of the Bastille on July 14, 1789 is often used to mark its start.

text: What empire was led by Genghis Khan in the 13th century?
hint: At its height, it was the largest contiguous land empire in history.

text: Who wrote the 95 Theses in 1517, challenging the Catholic Church?
hint: His writings sparked the Protestant Reformation and led to Christianity's split.

text: What was the codename of the US project that developed the first nuclear weapons?
hint: It was led by J. Robert Oppenheimer and resulted in the bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

text: Who assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand in 1914, triggering WWI?
hint: He was a Bosnian Serb nationalist and member of the Black Hand organisation.

text: What was the name of the policy of racial segregation in South Africa?
hint: The word means 'separateness' in Afrikaans; it lasted officially from 1948 to 1991.

text: In what year did Christopher Columbus first reach the Americas?
hint: He landed in the Bahamas on October 12, 1492, believing he had reached Asia.

text: What was the name of the first successful powered airplane flight, made by the Wright Brothers?
hint: Orville and Wilbur Wright flew at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, on December 17, 1903.

text: Which Roman emperor converted to Christianity and issued the Edict of Milan?
hint: He issued the Edict of Milan in 313 AD, granting religious tolerance throughout the Roman Empire.

text: What war was fought between the Union and Confederate states of America?
hint: It lasted from 1861 to 1865 and primarily concerned the issue of slavery.

text: Who was the longest-reigning British monarch?
hint: She reigned from 1952 to 2022, a reign of 70 years.

text: What is the name of the ancient Egyptian writing system using pictures and symbols?
hint: The Rosetta Stone, discovered in 1799, was key to deciphering this system.

text: In what year did India gain independence from Britain?
hint: Independence was granted on August 15, 1947, though the partition into India and Pakistan caused mass displacement.

text: What was the name of the treaty that ended World War I?
hint: Signed in 1919, it imposed harsh reparations on Germany, which many historians cite as a factor in WWII.

text: Who built the Taj Mahal and why?
answer: Mughal emperor Shah Jahan, as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal
hint: Construction began in 1632 and was completed around 1653 in Agra, India.

text: What 19th-century disease killed millions and prompted John Snow's pioneering epidemiological study in London?
hint: Snow mapped cases to a water pump on Broad Street, linking contaminated water to the outbreak.

text: What is the main ingredient in traditional Japanese miso soup?
hint: It's typically combined with dashi stock and may include tofu, seaweed, and green onions.

text: What fruit is used to make guacamole?
hint: This creamy green fruit is technically a berry and is native to Mexico and Central America.

text: What country is credited with inventing pizza?
hint: Modern pizza originated in Naples, with the Margherita pizza named after Queen Margherita of Savoy in 1889.

text: What is the most expensive spice in the world by weight?
hint: It comes from the stigmas of the Crocus sativus flower and must be hand-harvested.

text: What cooking technique involves cooking food sous vide?
answer: Cooking vacuum-sealed food in a precisely controlled water bath at low temperature
hint: It's French for 'under vacuum' and allows chefs to cook food to exact doneness throughout.

text: What is the Japanese art of raw fish preparation called?
answer: Sashimi (or sushi if combined with rice)
hint: Sashimi is purely the sliced raw fish; sushi refers to the combination with seasoned rice.

text: What ingredient makes bread dough rise?
hint: Yeast produces carbon dioxide gas through fermentation, causing the dough to expand.

text: What is the main flavour of the French sauce Béarnaise?
hint: Béarnaise is a butter sauce made with egg yolks, shallots, white wine vinegar, and tarragon.

text: From which country does the dish paella originate?
hint: It is traditionally from the Valencia region and cooked in a wide, shallow pan over an open fire.

text: What is the primary ingredient in hummus?
hint: The word 'hummus' actually means 'chickpea' in Arabic.

text: What is the difference between jam and jelly?
answer: Jam contains crushed or whole fruit pieces; jelly is made from strained fruit juice with no solids
hint: Preserves are another category that contains whole or large chunks of fruit.

text: What type of pasta is shaped like little ears?
hint: The name means 'little ears' in Italian; it is traditional to the Puglia region of southern Italy.

text: What grain is used to make traditional risotto?
answer: Arborio rice (or other short-grain Italian rice like Carnaroli)
hint: Its high starch content is what gives risotto its characteristic creamy consistency.

text: What is the name of the sweet fried dough pastry popular in New Orleans?
hint: They are typically served hot and dusted with powdered sugar at cafés like Café Du Monde.

text: Which country is the largest producer of coffee in the world?
hint: Brazil has been the world's largest coffee producer for over 150 years.

text: What is the name of the edible seaweed wrap used in sushi rolls?
hint: It is made from dried and pressed red algae and has a distinct umami flavour.

text: What spice gives curry its distinctive yellow colour?
hint: The active compound curcumin is also being researched for anti-inflammatory properties.

text: What is the cooking term for browning the surface of meat using high heat?
hint: It creates the Maillard reaction, which produces complex flavours and a browned crust.

text: From which city does the Philly cheesesteak originate?
hint: Pat Olivieri is often credited with creating it in the 1930s; it features thinly sliced beef and melted cheese.

text: What is the name of the Mexican dish consisting of a corn tortilla topped with beans, meat, and cheese?
hint: Unlike a taco, the tortilla is flat and crispy, not folded.

text: What is Worcestershire sauce made from?
answer: Fermented anchovies, tamarind, molasses, vinegar, onions, garlic, and spices
hint: Despite its British name, many of its ingredients are from South Asia; it was created in Worcester, England.

text: What is the key difference between baking soda and baking powder?
answer: Baking soda is pure sodium bicarbonate; baking powder contains sodium bicarbonate plus an acid and starch
hint: Baking soda needs an acidic ingredient to activate; baking powder can work without one.

text: What plant does vanilla flavouring naturally come from?
hint: The vanilla beans are the seed pods of this tropical orchid, native to Mexico.

text: What is the Italian term for 'before the pasta course', referring to appetisers?
hint: The word literally means 'before the meal' in Italian.

text: Which cheese is traditionally used in a Caesar salad dressing?
hint: The dressing also traditionally contains anchovies, egg yolk, lemon juice, garlic, and Worcestershire sauce.

text: What is the name of the Korean fermented cabbage dish?
hint: It is often made with napa cabbage and Korean chili flakes and serves as a staple side dish.

text: What nut is used to make marzipan?
hint: Ground almonds are combined with sugar and sometimes egg whites to form the pliable confection.

text: What is the term for cooking food directly above or below a heat source?
answer: Grilling (above) or broiling (below in an oven)
hint: In the UK, broiling is called grilling regardless of whether heat comes from above or below.

text: What is the main alcoholic ingredient in a Margarita?
hint: The classic recipe combines tequila, triple sec (or Cointreau), and lime juice.

text: What fruit is a key ingredient in Worcestershire sauce and is also used in Indian cooking?
hint: This tart tropical fruit is also used in pad thai, chutneys, and many Southeast Asian dishes.

text: What is the name of the French cooking technique of slowly cooking food in fat at a low temperature?
hint: Duck confit (confit de canard) is a classic French dish prepared this way.

text: What percentage of the Earth's surface is covered by water?
hint: Despite covering over two-thirds of Earth, only about 3% of that water is fresh.

text: What common household item was originally marketed as a wallpaper cleaner before becoming a children's toy?
hint: Noah McVicker created the clay-like compound in the 1950s; a teacher repurposed it for classroom art.

text: What is the fear of long words technically called?
hint: Ironically, the name of this phobia is itself an extremely long word.

text: How many times can a piece of paper be folded in half?
answer: About 7 times with a standard sheet (theoretically infinite with a large enough sheet)
hint: The myth says you can't fold it more than 7 times; in practice, a very long sheet has been folded 13 times.

text: What is the name of the dot above the letters 'i' and 'j'?
hint: This tiny typographic mark has its own name; the term 'jot' (as in 'jot it down') is related to the same concept.

text: Which animal has the highest blood pressure of any living creature?
hint: Its heart must pump blood up its extremely long neck to reach its brain.

text: What is the only food that never expires?
hint: Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still edible.

text: What country consumes the most chocolate per capita?
hint: The Swiss consume about 10–12 kg of chocolate per person per year.

text: What is the name of the character who says 'To infinity and beyond!' in Toy Story?
hint: He is a space ranger action figure voiced by Tim Allen.

text: How many sides does a hexagon have?
hint: Honeybees naturally build hexagonal cells in their honeycombs due to the shape's structural efficiency.

text: What is the name of the chemical that makes chili peppers hot?
hint: It binds to pain receptors in the mouth, triggering a burning sensation without actual heat damage.

text: What year was the World Wide Web invented, and who invented it?
hint: He invented it while working at CERN, proposing it as a system for sharing information among researchers.

text: What is the only mammal naturally capable of sustained flight?
hint: Flying squirrels glide but cannot truly fly; bats are the only mammals with wings for powered flight.

text: How many strings does a standard guitar have?
hint: Standard tuning from low to high is E, A, D, G, B, E.

text: What colour is the blood of an octopus?
hint: It contains haemocyanin, a copper-based oxygen carrier, rather than the iron-based haemoglobin in human blood.

text: How many bones does an adult human have?
hint: Babies are born with about 270–300 bones; many fuse together during childhood and adolescence.

text: What was the first toy to be advertised on television?
hint: In 1952, the original kit used real potatoes; plastic bodies came later.

text: What colour is a mirror, actually?
hint: A perfect mirror would be perfectly white; real mirrors reflect green wavelengths most efficiently, giving a faint green tint.

text: What is the shortest war in history?
hint: It lasted between 38 and 45 minutes on August 27, 1896.

text: What is the name for the cracking sound made by knuckles?
hint: The sound is caused by the rapid collapse of gas bubbles in the synovial fluid of joints.

text: What is the word for a word that reads the same forwards and backwards?
hint: Examples include 'racecar', 'level', and 'madam'.

text: What is the collective noun for a group of flamingos?
hint: Other colourful collective nouns include a 'murder' of crows and an 'exaltation' of larks.

text: In what language was the original Dracula novel written?
hint: Bram Stoker's 1897 novel was written in English, despite its Transylvanian setting and Romanian Count.

text: What is the name of the longest-running animated TV show in history?
hint: It premiered on December 17, 1989 and has aired over 750 episodes across more than 35 seasons.

text: What is the most common element in the human body?
hint: By mass, oxygen makes up about 65% of the human body, largely because of water (H₂O) in our cells.

text: What is the name of the phobia of being watched by a duck?
hint: Coined humorously by Gary Larson in a Far Side cartoon; it refers to the fear that somewhere, a duck is watching you.

text: What mathematical concept did zero not have in ancient Roman numerals?
hint: Roman numerals have no symbol for zero, which created complications for arithmetic and record-keeping.

text: What is the name of the optical illusion where parallel lines appear to converge in the distance?
hint: Artists and architects use this principle deliberately; railroad tracks are a classic real-world example.

text: What country celebrates Christmas on January 7th instead of December 25th?
hint: Orthodox Christians follow the Julian calendar, which places Christmas 13 days later than the Gregorian calendar.

text: What is the approximate number of stars in the Milky Way galaxy?
hint: The true number is difficult to determine; our sun is just one of an estimated 250 billion on average.

text: What is the name of the global agreement signed in 2015 to limit climate change?
hint: Adopted at COP21, it aims to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.

text: Which classic board game involves buying and trading property on a grid-based city map?
hint: The original game was based on the streets of Atlantic City, New Jersey.

text: What is the name of the evolutionary theory proposed by Charles Darwin?
hint: Published in On the Origin of Species in 1859, it describes how species evolve over generations through differential survival.

text: What is the rarest blood type in humans?
hint: Only about 1% of the population has this blood type.

text: How many keys does a standard piano have?
hint: This includes 52 white keys and 36 black keys spanning over seven octaves.

text: What is the name of the tallest building in the world as of 2024?
hint: Located in Dubai, UAE, it stands at 828 metres (2,717 feet) tall.

text: What is the name for the process of a caterpillar transforming into a butterfly?
hint: Inside the chrysalis, the caterpillar's body largely dissolves and is completely reorganised.

text: How many players are on a standard soccer (football) team on the field?
hint: This includes the goalkeeper; each team starts with 11 players, and substitutions are limited.

text: What is the name of the currency used in Japan?
hint: Japan is one of few developed economies that uses a currency with such a low unit value that goods cost hundreds to thousands of yen.

text: What word describes a word that is spelled the same forwards and backwards but means different things?
answer: That would still be a palindrome; there is no distinct term for same-spelled different-meaning palindromes
hint: The word 'level' is a palindrome; context distinguishes meanings if a word has multiple.

text: What is the name of the mythical creature that is half-human and half-horse?
hint: In Greek mythology, centaurs were wild creatures; Chiron was a notable exception, being wise and educated.

text: What is the term for an angle that measures exactly 90 degrees?
hint: It forms a perfect L-shape and is the basis of much of geometry, architecture, and construction.

text: What type of dance is associated with Argentina and Uruguay, typically danced in pairs in close embrace?
hint: It originated in the working-class neighbourhoods of Buenos Aires and Montevideo in the late 19th century.

text: What is the name of the fictional superhero team in the animated series Teen Titans?
hint: The team includes Robin, Starfire, Raven, Beast Boy, and Cyborg.

text: What is the name for the phenomenon where a black hole bends light around it?
hint: Einstein predicted this in general relativity; it was confirmed during the 1919 solar eclipse.

text: What is the pH level of pure water?
hint: Solutions below 7 are acidic; solutions above 7 are basic (alkaline).

text: What is the capital of Norway?
hint: Situated at the head of the Oslofjord, it is the most densely populated municipality in Norway.

text: What river flows through London?
hint: At 346 km, it is the longest river entirely within England.

text: What is the name of the famous canal that connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans?
hint: It opened in 1914 and cuts through the Isthmus of Panama, saving ships thousands of kilometres.

text: Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
hint: Including its islands, Canada's coastline stretches over 202,000 kilometres.

text: What is the capital of Indonesia?
hint: Indonesia is in the process of moving its capital to a newly built city on Borneo called Nusantara.

text: What is the name of the narrow strip of land connecting North and South America?
hint: It separates the Caribbean Sea from the Pacific Ocean and is the site of the Panama Canal.

text: What African country was formerly known as Rhodesia?
hint: It was renamed Zimbabwe in 1980 when Robert Mugabe led the country to independence.

text: In which country would you find the ancient ruins of Machu Picchu?
hint: Built by the Inca Empire in the 15th century, it sits at 2,430 metres above sea level in the Andes.

text: Who was the first Emperor of China?
hint: He unified China in 221 BCE and is also famous for the Terracotta Army buried near his tomb.

text: What was the name of the plague that devastated Europe in the 14th century?
hint: It killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe's population between 1347 and 1351.

text: In what year did the Soviet Union collapse?
hint: The USSR formally dissolved on December 25, 1991, when Mikhail Gorbachev resigned as president.

text: What ancient wonder was located at the harbour of the island of Rhodes?
hint: A massive statue of the sun god Helios, it stood for only 54 years before being destroyed by an earthquake.

text: Who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling?
hint: He painted it between 1508 and 1512 on a commission from Pope Julius II.

text: What was the name of the first US Space Shuttle to fly in space?
hint: It made its maiden flight in April 1981 and was tragically lost in 2003 during re-entry.

text: Which explorer is credited with being the first to sail around the world?
answer: Ferdinand Magellan (though Juan Sebastián Elcano completed the voyage)
hint: Magellan was killed in the Philippines; Elcano led the remaining crew back to Spain in 1522.

text: What was the name of the ship Charles Darwin sailed on during his voyages?
hint: The voyage from 1831 to 1836 took Darwin through South America and the Galápagos Islands.

text: What event marked the beginning of the Cold War era?
answer: The end of World War II and the emerging tensions between the USA and USSR (circa 1947)
hint: Winston Churchill's 'Iron Curtain' speech in 1946 is often cited as an early marker of the Cold War.

text: Who was the last Tsar of Russia?
hint: He abdicated in 1917 during the Russian Revolution and was executed with his family in 1918.

text: What ancient library was one of the largest and most significant in the ancient world?
hint: Located in Alexandria, Egypt, it aimed to collect all the world's knowledge and was burned or allowed to decay over time.

text: What year did the Korean War end with an armistice?
hint: The armistice was signed on July 27, 1953; technically, no peace treaty was ever signed.

text: Who was the first person to reach the South Pole?
hint: The Norwegian explorer reached the South Pole on December 14, 1911, just five weeks before Robert Falcon Scott.

text: What was the name of the first cloned mammal, produced in 1996?
hint: She was cloned from an adult somatic cell at the Roslin Institute in Scotland.

text: What American president issued the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863?
hint: It declared slaves in Confederate states to be free, though enforcement depended on Union military victory.

text: What was the name of the operation that was the Allied invasion of Normandy in 1944?
hint: The amphibious landings on June 6, 1944 were the largest seaborne invasion in history.

text: Who invented the telephone?
hint: He was awarded the first patent for the telephone in 1876, though Elisha Gray filed a caveat the same day.

text: What is the name of the Italian bread that is typically flat and seasoned with olive oil and herbs?
hint: It is often topped with rosemary, sea salt, and olives, and can be used as a sandwich base.

text: What is the primary protein in milk?
hint: Casein makes up about 80% of milk's protein content; whey is the other 20%.

text: What is the national dish of Spain?
answer: Paella (or tortilla española, depending on context)
hint: Paella is the most internationally recognised; tortilla española (potato omelette) is arguably more universally eaten daily.

text: What type of milk is traditionally used to make authentic mozzarella di bufala?
hint: The higher fat content gives it a richer, creamier texture than cow's milk mozzarella.

text: What is the name of the Brazilian slow-cooked black bean and pork stew?
hint: Considered Brazil's national dish, it is typically served with rice, collard greens, orange slices, and farofa.

text: What is the main ingredient in a traditional French bouillabaisse?
hint: This Provençal fish stew originated in the port city of Marseille.

text: What sweet condiment is made by bees from flower nectar?
hint: A single bee produces only about 1/12 of a teaspoon of honey in its lifetime.

text: What is the name for the thin, crispy outer layer formed when bread is baked?
hint: It forms due to the Maillard reaction and the evaporation of surface moisture during baking.

text: What type of pastry is used to make a croissant?
hint: Butter is repeatedly folded into the dough to create hundreds of flaky layers.

text: What is the name of the Japanese alcoholic beverage made from fermented rice?
hint: Sometimes called rice wine, it has an alcohol content of about 15–20%.

text: What is the key ingredient that distinguishes a Carbonara sauce from a cream-based pasta sauce?
hint: Authentic Roman Carbonara uses eggs, hard cheese, cured pork, and black pepper; cream is not traditional.

text: What part of the vanilla plant is used as the flavouring?
hint: The pods are long, thin, and dark brown; the seeds inside contain most of the flavour compounds.

text: What is the name of the dish made of thin slices of raw beef, typically served with olive oil and capers?
hint: Named after the Venetian painter Vittore Carpaccio by Harry's Bar in Venice; the colour resembled his paintings.

text: What grain is used to make polenta?
hint: It is a staple of northern Italian cuisine and can be served soft like porridge or cooled and sliced.

text: What is the Scoville scale used to measure?
hint: Bell peppers score 0 on the scale; the Carolina Reaper, once the world's hottest, exceeds 2 million Scoville units.

text: What country is credited with inventing ice cream?
answer: China (with Italian and Arab influences in its evolution)
hint: The Tang Dynasty in China consumed a frozen milk and rice mixture; modern ice cream evolved through Arab sharbat and Italian gelato.

text: What is the name of the Indian bread that is cooked in a tandoor oven?
hint: It is typically made with white flour and sometimes flavoured with garlic or stuffed with cheese.

text: What is the main ingredient of the Middle Eastern dip baba ganoush?
hint: It is blended with tahini, olive oil, lemon juice, and garlic.

text: What French cooking term describes cooking vegetables until they are tender but still slightly firm?
answer: Al dente (Italian, but also used in French cooking) / à point
hint: Though 'al dente' is more commonly associated with pasta, it applies to vegetables too; the French equivalent is 'cuit à point'.

text: What is the name of the first commercially successful video game, released in 1972?
hint: Created by Atari, it simulated a game of table tennis and was initially installed as arcade machines.

text: How many teeth does an adult human have (including wisdom teeth)?
hint: Without wisdom teeth, most adults have 28. Wisdom teeth can be absent in some people.

text: What is the name of the phenomenon where the sky appears blue?
hint: Sunlight's shorter blue wavelengths scatter more than longer red wavelengths as they pass through the atmosphere.

text: What is the world's best-selling book of all time?
hint: Estimated at over 5 billion copies sold; the next best-sellers are far behind.

text: What is the name of the force that makes a gyroscope resist tilting?
hint: It's caused by the conservation of angular momentum; the same principle keeps a spinning top upright.

text: What language has the most native speakers in the world?
hint: With over 900 million native speakers, it far exceeds Spanish and English.

text: What is the name of the effect where you hear a pitch change as a sound source moves toward or away from you?
hint: The classic example is an ambulance siren that sounds higher-pitched as it approaches and lower as it recedes.
